title: US government targets Cop City protester over phone operating system
excerpt: Concern over US effort to prosecute Sam Tunick, accused by authorities of wiping his phone using GrapheneOS
content_html:
The US Department of Justice is attempting to prosecute an Atlanta resident in connection with the movement against the police training center known as Cop City because he had GrapheneOS on his phone, an open-source operating system that enables users to enter a passcode and wipe a phone clean.

The case, which had its first hearing on Monday, centers on a little-known US federal statute that makes it a crime to destroy property in an effort to prevent it from being seized.

Experts said it may be the first time the law has been aimed at the operating system, which works on Google Pixel phones, and expressed concerns about a technology created for privacy and security being used to criminalize protesters.

“It’s concerning – and sends the message that [GrapheneOS] is criminal by default,” said Christophe Boutry, a cybersecurity and surveillance expert. Boutry and Bill Buddington, senior staff technologist at the Electronic Frontier Foundation, both said they had not seen a similar case.

The defendant, Sam Tunick, was stopped for interrogation at Atlanta’s Hartsfield-Jackson airport on 24 January last year, after vacationing in the Dominican Republic. Unbeknown to him, federal authorities had put him on a terrorism watchlist because of his alleged association with the movement against Cop City.

Opposition to the $109m police training center, which opened last spring, came from a wide range of local and national organizations and protesters, centered on concerns around police militarization and clearing forests in an era of climate crisis. Atlanta police said the center was needed for “world-class” training and to attract new officers.

Several state attempts to prosecute Cop City protesters have foundered in the last several years, while this is the second recent federal effort, after the justice department announced another indictment last month.

The case began shortly after Tunick got off a plane in Atlanta, returning from a vacation spent whale-watching and enjoying nature on a friend’s boat. He did not know that a homeland security agent had circulated an email three hours before. The email contained Tunick’s name and photo and indicated he was being investigated for “suspected terrorism activities”. It was sent to agents on the Customs and Border Patrol tactical terrorism response team and an FBI Atlanta joint-terrorism taskforce officer.

When Tunick arrived in Atlanta, an agent took him to a room with a table, several chairs and a camera and microphone, and several agents began a “secondary inspection”. But all their questions were centered on child sexual abuse images.

These and other details were revealed in Monday’s hearing, held for a judge to hear testimony on a motion filed by Tunick’s federal public defenders, asking the court to suppress all evidence the agents obtained, including the erasure of the phone’s contents after he gave them a passcode, due to violations of his constitutional rights.

The interrogation, including the questions about child sexual abuse images, was “a pretext for a fishing expedition into Mr Tunick’s connections” to the movement against Cop City, according to the motion.

Tunick asked four times during the interrogation to speak with a lawyer, but was rebuffed each time, according to courtroom testimony. Additionally, the agents produced no warrant and did not read Tunick his rights.

But a justice department attorney and the airport agents testifying on Monday asserted Tunick was merely subjected to an everyday interrogation at an international airport, “looking for anything that’s prohibited”, as Larry Findley, a CBP officer, said.

Agent Findley and several others repeatedly asked Tunick to open his phone during the interrogation, telling him they would seize it if he did not. When he finally provided a passcode, “the screen went blank, flashed several times and the phone appeared to restart”, according to the motion.

The motion filed on Tunick’s behalf draws into focus the question of which constitutional rights apply and which ones are suspended at national borders, including international airports.

A judge’s decision on the motion will take at least until end of October.

Marlon Kautz, a member of the Atlanta Solidarity Fund, said: “We all have a right to secure our private data against unconstitutional searches. And we should – especially in a time of rising authoritarianism.”

Meanwhile, Boutry, who lives in France, said Tunick’s case was of a piece with tendencies in France and Spain, where authorities have been frustrated in attempts to gain access to the phones of journalists, lawyers and political opponents due to GrapheneOS.

In Catalonia, Spain, police have been profiling people with Google Pixel phones, assuming they have GrapheneOS installed and are drug dealers or gang members.

At the same time, Boutry said, the “main goal [of the operating system] is protection of privacy”.

“They’re our phones and the state can’t tell us how to use them.”

sentences.en: Tunick asked four times during the interrogation to speak with a lawyer, but was rebuffed each time, according to courtroom testimony.
sentences.zh: 根据法庭证词，图尼克在审讯期间四次要求与律师交谈，但每次都被拒绝。

sentences.en: Additionally, the agents produced no warrant and did not read Tunick his rights.
sentences.zh: 此外，特工们没有出示逮捕令，也没有宣读图尼克的权利。

blocks.html_en: But a justice department attorney and the airport agents testifying on Monday asserted Tunick was merely subjected to an everyday interrogation at an international airport, “looking for anything that’s prohibited”, as Larry Findley, a CBP officer, said.

sentences.en: But a justice department attorney and the airport agents testifying on Monday asserted Tunick was merely subjected to an everyday interrogation at an international airport, “looking for anything that’s prohibited”, as Larry Findley, a CBP officer, said.
sentences.zh: 但周一作证的司法部律师和机场工作人员声称，图尼克只是在国际机场接受日常审讯，“寻找任何违禁物品”，海关和边境保护局官员拉里·芬德利 (Larry Findley) 表示。

blocks.html_en: Agent Findley and several others repeatedly asked Tunick to open his phone during the interrogation, telling him they would seize it if he did not. When he finally provided a passcode, “the screen went blank, flashed several times and the phone appeared to restart”, according to the motion.

sentences.en: Agent Findley and several others repeatedly asked Tunick to open his phone during the interrogation, telling him they would seize it if he did not.
sentences.zh: 芬德利探员和其他几人在审讯过程中多次要求图尼克打开手机，并告诉他如果不这样做，他们就会没收手机。

sentences.en: When he finally provided a passcode, “the screen went blank, flashed several times and the phone appeared to restart”, according to the motion.
sentences.zh: 动议称，当他最终提供密码时，“屏幕一片空白，闪烁了几次，手机似乎重新启动了”。

blocks.html_en: The motion filed on Tunick’s behalf draws into focus the question of which constitutional rights apply and which ones are suspended at national borders, including international airports.

sentences.en: The motion filed on Tunick’s behalf draws into focus the question of which constitutional rights apply and which ones are suspended at national borders, including international airports.
sentences.zh: 代表图尼克提出的动议使人们关注哪些宪法权利适用以及哪些宪法权利在国界（包括国际机场）被暂停的问题。

blocks.html_en: A judge’s decision on the motion will take at least until end of October.

sentences.en: A judge’s decision on the motion will take at least until end of October.
sentences.zh: 法官对该动议的决定至少要到十月底才能做出。

blocks.html_en: Marlon Kautz, a member of the Atlanta Solidarity Fund, said: “We all have a right to secure our private data against unconstitutional searches. And we should – especially in a time of rising authoritarianism.”

sentences.en: Marlon Kautz, a member of the Atlanta Solidarity Fund, said: “We all have a right to secure our private data against unconstitutional searches.
sentences.zh: 亚特兰大团结基金成员马龙·考茨表示：“我们都有权保护我们的私人数据免遭违宪搜查。

sentences.en: And we should – especially in a time of rising authoritarianism.”
sentences.zh: 我们应该这样做——尤其是在威权主义抬头的时代。”

blocks.html_en: Meanwhile, Boutry, who lives in France, said Tunick’s case was of a piece with tendencies in France and Spain, where authorities have been frustrated in attempts to gain access to the phones of journalists, lawyers and political opponents due to GrapheneOS.

sentences.en: Meanwhile, Boutry, who lives in France, said Tunick’s case was of a piece with tendencies in France and Spain, where authorities have been frustrated in attempts to gain access to the phones of journalists, lawyers and political opponents due to GrapheneOS.
sentences.zh: 与此同时，居住在法国的布特里表示，图尼克的案件与法国和西班牙的趋势有关，这两个国家的当局试图通过 GrapheneOS 获取记者、律师和政治对手的电话，但遭到挫败。

blocks.html_en: In Catalonia, Spain, police have been profiling people with Google Pixel phones, assuming they have GrapheneOS installed and are drug dealers or gang members.

sentences.en: In Catalonia, Spain, police have been profiling people with Google Pixel phones, assuming they have GrapheneOS installed and are drug dealers or gang members.
sentences.zh: 在西班牙加泰罗尼亚，警方一直在对使用 Google Pixel 手机的人进行分析，假设他们安装了 GrapheneOS，并且是毒贩或帮派成员。

blocks.html_en: At the same time, Boutry said, the “main goal [of the operating system] is protection of privacy”.

sentences.en: At the same time, Boutry said, the “main goal [of the operating system] is protection of privacy”.
sentences.zh: 与此同时，布特里表示，“（操作系统的）主要目标是保护隐私”。

blocks.html_en: “They’re our phones and the state can’t tell us how to use them.”

sentences.en: “They’re our phones and the state can’t tell us how to use them.”
sentences.zh: “它们是我们的手机，国家无法告诉我们如何使用它们。”

content_html_zh:
美国司法部正试图起诉一名与反对名为 Cop City 的警察培训中心的运动有关的亚特兰大居民，因为他的手机上装有 GrapheneOS，这是一种开源操作系统，允许用户输入密码并清除手机上的数据。

该案于周一举行了首次听证会，其核心是一项鲜为人知的美国联邦法规，该法规将破坏财产以防止其被扣押的行为定为犯罪行为。

专家表示，这可能是该法律首次针对适用于 Google Pixel 手机的操作系统，并对为隐私和安全而创建的技术被用来将抗议者定为犯罪表示担忧。

网络安全和监控专家 Christophe Boutry 表示：“这令人担忧，并传达了这样的信息：[GrapheneOS] 默认是犯罪行为。” 布特里和电子前沿基金会的高级技术专家比尔·巴丁顿都表示，他们没有见过类似的案例。

去年1月24日，被告萨姆·图尼克(Sam Tunick)在多米尼加共和国度假后在亚特兰大哈茨菲尔德-杰克逊机场被拦截审问。 他不知道的是，联邦当局已将他列入恐怖主义观察名单，因为他涉嫌参与反对警察城的运动。

去年春天耗资 1.09 亿美元投入使用的警察培训中心遭到了众多地方和国家组织以及抗议者的反对，主要是对气候危机时代警察军事化和森林砍伐的担忧。 亚特兰大警方表示，该中心需要进行“世界级”培训并吸引新警官。

过去几年里，各州起诉警察城抗议者的几次尝试均以失败告终，而这是继司法部上个月宣布另一项起诉书后，联邦最近的第二次起诉。

图尼克在亚特兰大下飞机后不久就开始了此案，当时他正在朋友的船上观鲸并享受大自然的假期回来。 他不知道国土安全部特工在三小时前散发了一封电子邮件。 该电子邮件包含图尼克的姓名和照片，并表明他正在因“涉嫌恐怖主义活动”而接受调查。 它被发送给海关和边境巡逻队战术恐怖主义反应小组的特工以及联邦调查局亚特兰大联合恐怖主义特遣部队的官员。

当图尼克抵达亚特兰大时，一名特工把他带到一个房间，里面有一张桌子、几把椅子以及一个摄像头和麦克风，几名特工开始了“二次检查”。 但他们所有的问题都集中在儿童性虐待图像上。

这些细节和其他细节在周一的听证会上披露，法官听取了图尼克联邦公设辩护人提出的一项动议的证词，该动议要求法院压制特工获得的所有证据，包括在他给他们密码后删除手机内容，因为侵犯了他的宪法权利。

根据该动议，这次审讯，包括有关儿童性虐待图像的问题，是“调查图尼克先生与反对警察城运动的关系的借口”。

根据法庭证词，图尼克在审讯期间四次要求与律师交谈，但每次都被拒绝。 此外，特工们没有出示逮捕令，也没有宣读图尼克的权利。

但周一作证的司法部律师和机场工作人员声称，图尼克只是在国际机场接受日常审讯，“寻找任何违禁物品”，海关和边境保护局官员拉里·芬德利 (Larry Findley) 表示。

芬德利探员和其他几人在审讯过程中多次要求图尼克打开手机，并告诉他如果不这样做，他们就会没收手机。 动议称，当他最终提供密码时，“屏幕一片空白，闪烁了几次，手机似乎重新启动了”。

代表图尼克提出的动议使人们关注哪些宪法权利适用以及哪些宪法权利在国界（包括国际机场）被暂停的问题。

法官对该动议的决定至少要到十月底才能做出。

亚特兰大团结基金成员马龙·考茨表示：“我们都有权保护我们的私人数据免遭违宪搜查。 我们应该这样做——尤其是在威权主义抬头的时代。”

与此同时，居住在法国的布特里表示，图尼克的案件与法国和西班牙的趋势有关，这两个国家的当局试图通过 GrapheneOS 获取记者、律师和政治对手的电话，但遭到挫败。

在西班牙加泰罗尼亚，警方一直在对使用 Google Pixel 手机的人进行分析，假设他们安装了 GrapheneOS，并且是毒贩或帮派成员。

与此同时，布特里表示，“（操作系统的）主要目标是保护隐私”。

“它们是我们的手机，国家无法告诉我们如何使用它们。”
